md: **Trig identities** are equations involving trig functions that are true for *every* angle. They're tools â€” knowing them lets you simplify ugly expressions, solve equations more cleverly, prove other identities, and integrate functions in calculus. Memorize a small core set; the rest follow.

blocks.title: The Pythagorean identity
blocks.body: $$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$$ True for every $\theta$. Comes directly from the unit circle: every point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$.

md: Two cousin identities follow from dividing the Pythagorean identity by $\cos^2\theta$ and by $\sin^2\theta$:
These are just renaming the inverses of the basic three:
Cosine is **even** â€” symmetric across the y-axis. Sine and tangent are **odd** â€” they flip sign with the input:
Use the unit-circle widget to confirm: at $\theta = 60Â°$ vs $\theta = -60Â°$ (or $300Â°$), the cosine values are equal but the sines are opposites.
These let you find $\sin(A + B)$ and $\cos(A + B)$ when you know $\sin A, \cos A, \sin B, \cos B$ â€” and they're the foundation for most other identities.

blocks.title: Memory tip
blocks.body: Sine sum/difference: '**sin cos plus/minus cos sin**' â€” same operations, same letters. Cosine sum/difference: '**cos cos minus/plus sin sin**' â€” note the **opposite** sign! (Plus inside â†’ minus outside; minus inside â†’ plus outside.)

md: ### Worked example â€” exact value of $\sin 75Â°$
Now you can write $\sin 75Â°$ in **exact form** without a calculator!
From $\tan = \sin/\cos$ applied to the sum/difference identities:

blocks.tex: \tan(A + B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B} \qquad \tan(A - B) = \dfrac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A \tan B}

md: Special cases of the sum identities with $A = B$:

blocks.title: Three forms of cos(2Î¸)
blocks.body: $\cos(2\theta)$ has three equivalent forms â€” pick the one that matches what you have. **$\cos^2 - \sin^2$**: useful when both are present. **$1 - 2\sin^2\theta$**: useful if you want everything in terms of sine. **$2\cos^2\theta - 1$**: useful if you want everything in terms of cosine. The last two are also the building blocks for the half-angle identities below.

md: Take square roots (the sign depends on which quadrant $\theta/2$ is in):
$15Â° = 30Â°/2$. Use the half-angle identity for cosine:
(Sign is positive because $15Â°$ is in Q I, where cosine is positive.)
**'Prove the identity'** problems give you an equation to verify. The standard methodology is a craft. Some moves to try:

blocks.title: Strategy for proving identities
blocks.body: **Pick one side** and transform it step by step until you arrive at the other side. (Don't manipulate both sides as if they were equal â€” that assumes what you're trying to prove.) **Pick the more complicated side** to start with â€” you have more to break down. **Substitute identities aggressively**: replace $\tan, \cot, \sec, \csc$ with their $\sin/\cos$ definitions. **Combine fractions, find common denominators, factor.** **When stuck, try the Pythagorean identity** in some form.

md: Start with the LHS. Multiply numerator and denominator by the *conjugate* of the denominator, $1 + \cos\theta$:
(Faster route: divide $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ by $\cos^2$ to get $\tan^2 + 1 = \sec^2$, i.e. $\sec^2 - \tan^2 = 1$. Both methods work â€” but writing it from $\sin/\cos$ first is the safer default.)
Start with the *more complex* RHS. Substitute $\tan = \sin/\cos$ and $1 + \tan^2 = \sec^2 = 1/\cos^2$:

blocks.title: When in doubt â€” go to sin and cos
blocks.body: If a proof feels stuck, rewrite everything in terms of $\sin$ and $\cos$ using the reciprocal and quotient identities. From there, find common denominators and apply the Pythagorean identity. This 'fundamental form' approach succeeds 90% of the time.

md: Less commonly memorized but useful for integration and Fourier work:
These are derived directly from the sum/difference identities by adding or subtracting pairs.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Memorize the **Pythagorean** ($\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$), **quotient** ($\tan = \sin/\cos$), and **angle-sum** formulas. From sum-with-$A = B$ comes **double-angle**; from solving cos double-angle for sinÂ˛ or cosÂ˛ comes **half-angle**. To **prove** an identity, pick one side (preferably the messier one) and rewrite step by step using definitions, common denominators, and Pythagorean. Identities are *tools* â€” you build fluency by using them, not by listing them.
